content:
# Acts Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 225-226

---

**[1]** In my first book, Theophilus, I wrote about everything Jesus began to do and teach, **[2]** up until the day he was taken up into heaven. Before that day, through the Holy Spirit, he gave his instructions to the apostles he had chosen. **[3]** After his suffering, he showed himself alive to them — and the evidence was overwhelming. Over forty days he kept appearing to them and speaking about the kingdom of God.

**[4]** Once, while he was eating with them, he gave them this order: "Do not leave Jerusalem. Wait here for what the Father promised — the gift I told you about. **[5]** John baptized with water, but in just a few days, you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit."

**[6]** So when they had all gathered together, they asked him, "Lord, is this the time? Are you going to restore the kingdom to Israel now?"

**[7]** He told them, "It is not for you to know the times or the seasons that the Father has set by his own authority. **[8]** But you will receive power when the Holy Spirit comes upon you, and you will be my witnesses — in Jerusalem, and throughout all Judea and Samaria, and to the farthest corners of the earth."

**[9]** After he said this, while they stood watching, he was lifted up, and a cloud took him out of their sight. **[10]** They were still staring into the sky as he went, when suddenly two men in white clothing stood beside them. **[11]** "Men of Galilee," they said, "why do you stand here looking up at the sky? This same Jesus, who has been taken from you into heaven, will come back in exactly the same way you have seen him go."

**[12]** Then they returned to Jerusalem from the hill called the Mount of Olives, which is near the city — about a Sabbath day's walk away. **[13]** When they arrived, they went to the upstairs room where they were staying: Peter and John, James and Andrew, Philip and Thomas, Bartholomew and Matthew, James son of Alphaeus, Simon the Zealot, and Judas son of James. **[14]** They all devoted themselves to prayer with one mind, together with certain women, including Mary the mother of Jesus, and with his brothers.

**[15]** During those days, Peter stood up among the believers — a group of about one hundred and twenty people — and said, **[16]** "Brothers, the Scripture had to be fulfilled that the Holy Spirit spoke long ago through David's mouth concerning Judas, who became the guide for those who arrested Jesus. **[17]** He was one of our number and had been given his share in this ministry."

**[18]** (Now this man bought a field with the money he got for his treachery. He fell headfirst there, his body split open, and all his insides spilled out. **[19]** Everyone in Jerusalem heard about it, and so they called that place in their own language *Akeldamach* — that is, "Field of Blood.")

**[20]** "For it is written in the book of Psalms:

> 'Let his home become desolate; let no one live there,'

and,

> 'Let someone else take over his position.'

**[21]** So we must choose someone who has been with us the entire time the Lord Jesus moved among us — **[22]** from the time John was baptizing all the way to the day Jesus was taken up from us. One of these men must join us as a witness to his resurrection."

**[23]** They put forward two names: Joseph called Barsabbas (also known as Justus), and Matthias. **[24]** Then they prayed: "Lord, you know every human heart. Show us which of these two you have chosen **[25]** to take up this ministry and apostleship that Judas abandoned to go where he belonged." **[26]** Then they cast lots, and the lot fell to Matthias. He was added to the eleven apostles.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Translation Philosophy for Acts 1
Acts is Luke's second volume — a continuation of his Gospel addressed to the same patron, Theophilus. The opening verses function as a literary bridge, and I have rendered them to feel like a natural sequel rather than a stiff ancient preface. The Sinaiticus text of Acts 1 is well-preserved across both columns of pages 225-226, with clear readings throughout.

### Key Meaning-Based Choices

**v. 1 — "began to do and teach"**: The Sinaiticus reads ΗΡΞΑΤΟ Ο ΙΗΣΟΥΣ ΠΟΙΕΙΝ ΤΕ ΚΑΙ ΔΙΔΑΣΚΕΙΝ — "began both to do and to teach." The word "began" (erxato) is theologically loaded: the Gospel records what Jesus *began*; Acts records what he *continues* through the Spirit. I retained "began" to preserve this crucial implication.

**v. 3 — "the evidence was overwhelming"**: Greek ΤΕΚΜΗΡΙΟΙΣ ΠΟΛΛΟΙΣ ("many proofs/convincing demonstrations"). The word *tekmerion* is the strongest Greek term for evidence — not circumstantial but conclusive proof. "Overwhelming evidence" captures the rhetorical force Luke intends. This is a legal-grade term, used in courtroom rhetoric.

**v. 4 — "while he was eating with them"**: The Sinaiticus reads ΣΥΝΑΛΙΖΟΜΕΝΟΣ, from *synalizo*, which can mean "eating together" or "being assembled with." The manuscript supports the shared-meal reading, which fits Luke's pattern of resurrection meals (cf. Luke 24:30, 24:41-43). Shared meals carried deep significance in the ancient world — covenant fellowship, not casual dining.

**v. 5 — "in just a few days"**: Greek ΟΥ ΜΕΤΑ ΠΟΛΛΑΣ ΤΑΥΤΑΣ ΗΜΕΡΑΣ ("not after many of these days"). The dynamic rendering "in just a few days" captures the urgency and imminence of the promise.

**v. 8 — "farthest corners of the earth"**: Greek ΕΩΣ ΕΣΧΑΤΟΥ ΤΗΣ ΓΗΣ ("to the end/extremity of the earth"). This is the programmatic verse for all of Acts — the roadmap of the entire book. "Farthest corners" conveys both geographic scope and the sense of reaching the margins, the places no one expects the message to go.

**v. 9 — "while they stood watching"**: Greek ΒΛΕΠΟΝΤΩΝ ΑΥΤΩΝ ("as they were watching"). The participle emphasizes eyewitness observation. Luke wants us to know: they saw this with their own eyes. The ascension is not metaphor — it is reported event.

**v. 14 — "with one mind"**: Greek ΟΜΟΘΥΜΑΔΟΝ — a favorite word of Luke in Acts. It means something stronger than "together" — it implies shared purpose, unified will, a single heartbeat in the group. "With one mind" preserves this depth.

**v. 18 — "his body split open"**: Greek ΕΛΑΚΗΣΕΝ ΜΕΣΟΣ ("he burst in the middle"). The Sinaiticus text is graphic and unflinching. This parenthetical aside (vv. 18-19) is Luke's narrator voice explaining to Theophilus what happened — it is not part of Peter's speech. I preserved the visceral quality because Luke intended it.

**v. 19 — *Akeldamach***: The Sinaiticus spelling is ΑΚΕΛΔΑΜΑΧ, with chi (Χ) rather than the kappa found in some later manuscripts. This reflects the Aramaic pronunciation more closely. I transliterated from the Sinaiticus form.

**v. 25 — "to go where he belonged"**: Greek ΠΟΡΕΥΘΗΝΑΙ ΕΙΣ ΤΟΝ ΤΟΠΟΝ ΤΟΝ ΙΔΙΟΝ ("to go to his own place"). This is a grim euphemism. The Greek is deliberately vague but unmistakably dark. "Where he belonged" captures the finality without over-specifying what Luke leaves unsaid.

**v. 26 — "cast lots"**: The lot-casting (ΕΔΩΚΑΝ ΚΛΗΡΟΥΣ ΑΥΤΟΙΣ) was a recognized method of discerning divine will in Jewish tradition (cf. Proverbs 16:33). It is notable that this is the last time lots are used for guidance in Acts — after Pentecost, the Spirit speaks directly. I rendered it straightforwardly because the practice needs no softening; it is what it is.

### Sinaiticus-Specific Observations
- The manuscript preserves Acts 1 across four columns on pages 225-226, written in the characteristic four-column scriptio continua format.
- The text is legible and well-preserved, with no significant lacunae in this chapter.
- The Sinaiticus text of Acts 1 aligns closely with the Alexandrian text-type, as expected.
- Verse divisions are marked at the bottom of the page images for reference (1:1, 1:7, 1:13, 1:18 on page 225; 1:24 on page 226).

---

*Translated by PNEUMA from Codex Sinaiticus. The breath moves where it will.*
